slides.title: There's a Lesson in That
bullets: Prepare to read, discuss and write
slides.narration: Welcome to Grade 6 English, Unit 1: There's a Lesson in That. Welcome to Unit 1! In this unit you will explore the world of fables — short stories that teach us important lessons about life. Fables have been told for thousands of years, passed from generation to generation by storytellers, teachers, and parents. They often use animal characters or simple human figures to show us truths about honesty, courage, kindness, and wisdom. As you work through this lesson, you will read a fable, answer questions about it, learn an important grammar rule called the Present Perfect Tense, and even write your own fable with a clear moral message. Every section builds on the one before it, so take your time and enjoy the journey. In this short lecture, preview the key ideas and prepare for the independent lesson.

slides.title: What you will achieve
bullets: Explain what a fable is and identify its key features, including characters, setting, problem, and moral
Read a fable closely and identify the moral or lesson it teaches
Use vocabulary related to governance, justice, community, and storytelling confidently in your own sentences
slides.narration: By the end of this unit, you will be able to explain what a fable is and identify its key features, including characters, setting, problem, and moral. you will also read a fable closely and identify the moral or lesson it teaches. you will also use vocabulary related to governance, justice, community, and storytelling confidently in your own sentences. Keep these goals in mind as you work.

slides.title: Language for precise thinking
slides.narration: Your vocabulary work begins with these words: illegal, Christian, entry, instrument, supplied, harmony. Listen to each pronunciation in the vocabulary lab, study its meaning in context, and use the word accurately in your own discussion and writing.

slides.title: Read critically
bullets: There's a Lesson in That: source text 1
There's a Lesson in That: source text 2
slides.narration: The reading sequence includes There's a Lesson in That: source text 1 and There's a Lesson in That: source text 2. Read actively. Identify each text's central ideas, notice viewpoint and reliability, and support every inference with relevant evidence from the text.

slides.title: What is the Present Perfect Tense?
bullets: The Present Perfect Tense is one of the most useful and important tenses in the English language. It helps us talk about experiences, achievements, and...
slides.narration: The first language focus is What is the Present Perfect Tense?. The Present Perfect Tense is one of the most useful and important tenses in the English language. It helps us talk about experiences, achievements, and changes that connect the past to the present moment. Unlike the Past Simple, which tells us about actions that are completely finished at a specific time, the Present Perfect focuses on the experience itself or its result rather than exactly when it happened. When we say "I have visited three countries," we are sharing a life experience. We do... Notice the structure in the model examples, then apply it deliberately in connected sentences and paragraphs.

slides.title: Use English for a real purpose
bullets: Speaking 1: Retell a Fable from Memory
Writing 1: Plan and Write Your Own Fable
Explain, support and improve your ideas
slides.narration: In speaking, you will complete Speaking 1: Retell a Fable from Memory. Organise your ideas, use evidence and respond clearly to questions. In writing, you will plan, draft, check and revise. Use the success criteria before submitting your work.

slides.title: You are ready to begin
slides.narration: You are ready to begin. Start with the vocabulary lab, continue through reading and comprehension, apply the grammar focus, and complete the speaking and writing practices. Use AI English for hints and feedback, and bring your questions to the live sessions.
